rules.name: Hick's Law
rules.description: Decision time increases with the number of choices presented.
rules.application: Limit visible options; use progressive disclosure for complex choices.
rules.example: A pricing page with 3 tiers instead of 7 plans.
rules.anti_pattern: A navigation menu with 20+ top-level items.

rules.name: Fitts's Law
rules.description: The time to reach a target depends on its size and distance.
rules.application: Make primary actions large and place them near the user's current focus.
rules.example: Large CTA button placed directly below the value proposition text.
rules.anti_pattern: A tiny 'Submit' link in the corner of a large form.

rules.name: Miller's Law
rules.description: People can hold roughly 7 (plus or minus 2) items in working memory.
rules.application: Chunk information into groups of 5-9 related items.
rules.example: Navigation with 5 main categories, each with subcategories.
rules.anti_pattern: A flat list of 15 ungrouped settings on one page.

rules.name: Von Restorff Effect
rules.description: Distinct items in a group are more memorable.
rules.application: Make the desired action visually distinct from alternatives.
rules.example: One filled primary button among outlined secondary buttons.
rules.anti_pattern: All action buttons styled identically, none standing out.

rules.name: Serial Position Effect
rules.description: People remember the first and last items in a series best.
rules.application: Place the most important items at the beginning and end of lists.
rules.example: Most used nav items first and last in the navigation bar.
rules.anti_pattern: Burying the most important feature in the middle of a long menu.

rules.name: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
rules.description: Users perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as more usable.
rules.application: Invest in visual polish; it directly impacts perceived usability.
rules.example: Well-styled form with subtle shadows and smooth transitions.
rules.anti_pattern: A functional but visually raw interface with no styling attention.
